faers_data.reporting_trend: Stable; no new signal emergence in last 5 years
faers_data.note: Lactic acidosis signal is elevated and concentrated in patients with renal impairment. Absolute risk remains low (~8.4 cases per 100,000 patient-years) with proper contraindication adherence.

label_data.black_box_warning: LACTIC ACIDOSIS: Postmarketing cases of metformin-associated lactic acidosis have resulted in death, hypothermia, hypotension, and resistant bradyarrhythmias. Risk factors include renal impairment, hepatic impairment, congestive heart failure, and older age. Withhold for iodinated contrast imaging procedures.
label_data.mechanism_summary: Decreases hepatic glucose production via inhibition of mitochondrial complex I; increases peripheral glucose uptake; does not stimulate insulin secretion.
contraindications: Active or unstable congestive heart failure
label_data.label_revision_note: FDA updated prescribing information in 2016 to allow use in patients with eGFR 30-60 with dose adjustment; lactic acidosis risk language refined.

signal_analysis.method: Multi-item Gamma Poisson Shrinker (MGPS) + Proportional Reporting Ratio
signal_analysis.benefit_risk_assessment: Benefit-risk remains FAVORABLE. Signal is real but manageable through existing label contraindications and monitoring. No market action warranted.
signal_analysis.recommendation: MONITOR — existing contraindications and labeling are adequate. Enhanced monitoring for renal impairment patients recommended.

comparators.note: Predecessor drug; withdrawn 1977 due to lactic acidosis

mechanism_data.primary_mechanism: Inhibition of mitochondrial complex I of the respiratory chain → reduced oxidative phosphorylation → decreased hepatic gluconeogenesis
mechanism_data.lactic_acidosis_mechanism: Complex I inhibition → shift from oxidative to anaerobic metabolism → pyruvate accumulates → converted to lactate → lactic acidosis, particularly when hepatic lactate clearance is impaired

pharmacokinetics.distribution: Not protein bound; accumulates in intestinal wall, liver, kidneys
pharmacokinetics.elimination: Renal excretion unchanged (no hepatic metabolism) — explains risk in renal impairment

mechanism_data.clinical_relevance: Lactic acidosis is mechanistically plausible, confirmed in clinical practice, and dose-dependent in renally impaired patients.

key_studies.title: Risk of lactic acidosis with metformin use in type 2 diabetes: a systematic review
key_studies.finding: Absolute risk remains low (~8.4 per 100,000 patient-years) with proper contraindication adherence. Could not confirm excess risk vs comparators.

key_studies.title: Metformin-associated lactic acidosis: Current perspectives
key_studies.finding: Regulatory monitoring is adequate; no market withdrawal recommended. Most cases occur in contraindicated patients.

literature_data.regulatory_precedent: EMA and FDA both reviewed the metformin lactic acidosis signal in 2016 and concluded monitoring is sufficient. Neither agency recommended market restriction or withdrawal.
literature_data.expert_consensus: International conference on harmonisation recommends enhanced renal monitoring, not market action.
